videos.transcript: I've helped millions of tourists enjoy their visits to NYC and in this video I'm sharing the best 3-day itinerary on YouTube so you can enjoy this amazing City with zero stress but before we drive or fly to NYC there are three things you absolutely need to know before arriving starting with checking the weather we have had such wild weather swings the last few years unless it's Peak summer you never know what you're going to get here I definitely pack an umbrella and possible extra layers even when it's hot because the air conditioning blasts here and for winter check the weekly forecast before you arrive to see just how cold you need to pack for bring some cash New York is not like London where I didn't touch cash the entire trip at little pizza shops in Chinatown there are still cash only places and even in small delies and supermarkets I have seen minimums of $10 to use a credit card so carrying at least $50 to $100 in cash is never a bad idea here stay alert while New York is one of the safest large cities in America the city still has issues with mental illness and drug use so always be aware of your surroundings especially when riding the subway we'll keep this simple and concentrate your first day in Midtown Manhattan the heartbeat of the city for breakfast a classic American experience at the Tik Tock Diner right across from Penn Station you've probably seen this spot in movies it's the classic American Diner open 24 hours a day this place serves up some seriously massive plates of pancakes and French toast you could easily split one of these between two people breakfast starts at just $9.95 for two eggs any style and prices go up to around 20 bucks for some of the bigger dishes it's a Midtown staple and a total viewer favorite after you finish there's a little Hidden Gem in the basement of the New Yorker Hotel next door a tiny Museum dedicated to Nicola Tesla it's only worth about 5 minutes but definitely check it out it's where the famous inventor spent the last years of his life you're less than a 10-minute walk to Bryant Park from late October to early March this place transforms into the winter village hands down the best holiday market in the city but even when it's not the holiday season it's still one of my favorite parks to just chill perfect spot to relax at a table people watch and soak in that classic New York Vibe oh and here's a little bonus it's got the cleanest public restroom in the Big Apple you could practically eat off the floor in there right next to Brian Park is the New York Public Library and honestly this might be one of the best free things to do in the city my wife and I love exploring this place between the reading rooms and the stunning hallways it feels like you're walking through free museum and speaking of museums the palansky exhibition on the ground floor is like its own mini Museum you can see the original winie of the Poo the Declaration of Independence and so much more on display and that's just the beginning you could easily spend 30 minutes here just soaking it all in at this point it's time you experience one of the city's best tourist attractions and observation deck for first timers my favorite observation deck is definitely Top of the Rock if you're looking for stunning views on a beautiful day this is the spot at 70 floors up it's the most intimate observation deck putting you right in the heart of Midtown Manhattan plus with tickets starting at just 40 bucks it's one of the best value options out there and don't miss their newest attractions beam and Sky lift beam lets you feel what it was like to be a construction worker on lunch break and it's a little scary if you're afraid of heights fair warning after taking in the views head over to Grand Central Terminal New York's most beautiful Transit Hub snap some iconic shots in the main hall featuring The breathtaking Celestial ceiling mural then take a quick detour next door to the Ford Atrium Garden Hidden Gem alert this massive Greenhouse maintains tropical conditions for plants to flourish year round with real rainwater collected straight from the roof it's the perfect peaceful Escape in the middle of the city for lunch you're eating the most iconic sandwich in New York pastrami on Ry with mustard Sarge's Deli has won every contest we've ever had comparing it to Cates and all the other heavy weight and it's not swarm with tourists either nope this is a local spot this would be a moment to consider splitting one for two people especially if you had a big breakfast at the diner the pastrami is Juicy and you'll leave satisfied that I can promise you after lunch Uber or Subway up to Central Park start at 59th Street and Columbus Circle you could easily spend an entire afternoon exploring Central Park trust me I've done it it stretches over 50 blocks but as a first timer you really only need to soak in a few key spots start with sheep Meadow for stunning Skyline views over the lawn then head over to Bethesda Terrace walk through the arcade to admire the beautiful archways and the world's only ceiling made entirely of mint and tiles don't miss the fountain and nearby Pond always a peaceful site if you're short on time I filmed the Central Park guide recently that shows how to see the best of the park in Just 2 hours check that one out for more ideas the American Museum of Natural History is a must for any visitor and their new Gilder Center is a game changer especially for families open less than 2 years ago this stunning addition features immersive exhibits that bring science and nature to life kids will love the visible world's exhibit a mind-blowing interactive experience and the impressive butterfly vivarium where you can walk among hundreds of live butterflies plus the museums like conic dinosaur fossils and massive blue whale model make it one of the coolest spots to explore in NYC all right it's time to go rest up and head back to Midtown for dinner and we'll be at John's of Time Square this isn't just any pizza spot it's the only one set inside a stunning old church located just a few blocks from Time Square it's the perfect stop if you've got PL to catch a Broadway show afterward and the pizza absolutely delicious after dinner make your way to Time Square and take it all in start at father Duffy Square near the big red steps this is probably the most iconic spot in the United States for snapping a photo but don't linger too long this area is also Prime territory for tourist scams we've covered this in depth in another video but here's the quick version if someone hands you a CD a bracelet or if Spider-Man insists on a photo keep walking and don't make eye contact Trust trust me you'll thank me later for some affordable souvenirs head to Grand Slam New York they've got all the I Love New York shirts keychains and trinkets you could want and the prices are surprisingly budget friendly if you're ready to relax so it'll head over to beast and butterflies inside the M social Hotel out of all the rooftop bars I've covered in the past 5 years this one has gotten the most buzz from my viewers and for good reason the views of Time Square are incredible the food and drinks are delicious and here's the kicker kids are actually welcome you is an understatement it's the perfect way to end your night in Time Square today we're diving into Lower Manhattan or as the locals call it downtown let's kick things off with breakfast Pearl Diner this place takes me right back to the diners I grew up with across the Hudson in New Jersey it's a classic hole in the wall that's been serving locals for over 50 years with $10 to $20 you'll get plenty of food and the nostalgic Vibes are unbeatable we'll begin at one of the most somber and meaningful memorials in the city the 9/11 Memorial dedicated to the day New York and the world Chang Forever on September 11th 2001 The Fountains Mark the footprints of where the Twin Towers once stood it's a place for quiet reflection so please save The Smiling selfies for elsewhere the Oculus is one of the cleanest and most futuristic Transit hubs in New York it feels like you've stepped inside a spaceship it's also home to a high-end mall but even if shopping isn't on your list it's worth stopping by for a photo because the architecture is just that stunning fun fact the Oculus has a skylight that opens every year on on September 11th allowing sunlight to stream through in a powerful tribute to the Twin Towers a trip to Liberty Island in the Statue of Liberty is a must but here's an important tip do not buy tickets from vendors hanging around the area they're often overpriced or scammed instead book your tickets in advance especially if you want to experience the best view climbing inside the crown of the Statue of Liberty Crown access is limited and requires reservations months ahead so plan early this visit will take up the rest of your morning and likely stretch into the afternoon while you're there consider stopping for lunch at the cafe on Ellis Island which offers a convenient break before exploring more fun fact Ellis Island was the entry point for over 12 million immigrants between 1892 and 1954 making it a place of deep history and connection for many families once you're back in Manhattan take a stroll down Stone Street its Charming cobblestones were recreated to reflect how New York looked back in the 1600s this historic Street was home to the city's first breweries and today you can still grab a beer or a bite at one of the many cozy restaurants lining the block it almost feels like You' stepped into a scene straight out of Harry Potter all right it's time to regroup we'll walk Uber or Subway to Chinatown starting at Deer Street welcome to the most instagrammable Street in Manhattan this spot is also home to the infamous bloody angle a sharp Bend in the road with a dark past it was once a notorious site for gang rivalries and assassinations by Chinese gangs today though it's a much friendlier Place known for some of the best Chinese food in New York who's ready for a late lunch we didn't eat at Ellis Island Deluxe Greenbow this spot is a local favorite for the best soup dumplings in Manhattan with communal seating it's everything you'd hope for in an authentic Chinatown experience fast service and Incredibly delicious food don't miss a steam pork and crab mixed tiny bun starting at $9.95 heads up it's cash or venmo only so come prepared if you've got any room left for dessert head across the street to the original Chinatown Ice Cream Factory a neighborhood staple they're known for unique and exotic flavors like leachi and black sesame so come with an open mind and a sweet tooth ready to try something new and if you're looking to explore more of Chinatown we've got you covered check out our Full Compilation video packed with hours of food and activity recommendations time to walk off that food coma or Subway taxi if you're too tired to grenwich Village head north and make your way to Washington Square Park a longtime cultural Hot Spot it's the perfect place for people watching and you're almost guaranteed to catch some live music or street [Music] performances here's a fun little Easter egg if you look straight through the Washington Square Arch you'll get a picture perfect view of the Empire State Building from there head to 7th Avenue walk south and turn right onto Grove Street that's where you'll find the exterior of the friends apartment building the surrounding area is absolutely stunning filled with classic Brownstones and some of the most expensive Real Estate in Manhattan walk up to the Highline if you're feeling extra ambitious one of New York's most famous free parks this elevated walkway was once an old freight train track but has been transformed into a stunning Urban green space as you stroll you'll pass impressive art murals beautiful landscaping and get unique views of the city with streets and cars below you don't have to walk the entire 1 and 1/2 miles but it's definitely worth snapping some photos along the way fun fact the Highland was inspired by a similar elevated Park in Paris called prominade Plante if you follow it North you can end your walk at Hudson yards where you'll find the iconic vessel a futuristic honeycomb like structure perfect for a quick photo take a break you've earned it because for dinner we're heading to Korea Town or K Town as the locals call it if you're looking to check out Korea Town I would head to food Gallery 32 it's my wife Adriana's and my favorite spot for quick delicious Korean food kobu is our pick our go-to is the barbecue rice bowl with pork but there's plenty of variety to choose from plus if you're in the mood for something else the food court has a ton of other vendors to explore making it a perfect stop for a casual meal after dinner make a stop at Rudy's bar and grill in Hell's Kitchen this is hands down my favorite Dive Bar in the city and it's been around since 1933 the beer is cheap their blonde a is Just 4 bucks and every drink comes with a free hot dog Yes you heard that right a free hot dog it's a throwback to prices from the early 2000s and the vibe is unbeatable a perfect mix of local characters and curious tourists time for day three of your New York trip and if you're looking for something quick but iconic for breakfast Russ and Daughters is one of those classic New York institutions that truly lives up to the hype for a more relaxed experience headed just just a few blocks over to their Cafe for a sitdown version of the same legendary food can't go wrong with their classic bagel and lock sandwich no it's not cheap but yes it's absolutely worth every penny then we're going to take a Subway taxi or Uber to pier 11 Wall Street here's a tip most guides won't mention take the NYC Ferry for just $4 download the NYC Ferry app to pay easily by phone as you cross the East River you'll get unbeatable views of the Brooklyn Bridge and in about 5 minutes you'll land right in the heart of dumb much closer than taking the subway which involves a longer walk if you've got extra time and want to discover more ways to use the ferry check out my video where I break it all down I encourage you to stroll around Brooklyn Bridge Park for all the photo opportunities it's truly one of the most beautiful areas of the city while you're in the area especially if you've got little ones make sure to stop by Jane's Carousel I'd argue there isn't a carousel in the world with a better backdrop this beautifully restored wooden Carousel built in 1922 features 48 hand carved horses and is truly a masterpiece if you've seen that iconic photo of the Manhattan Bridge framed perfectly between buildings it's taken right here at the corner of Washington and Water Street I won't blame you for snapping the shop but be prepared to jockey for position with other photographers or you know just Photoshop everyone out later for lunch we'll keep things simple and send you to the touristy timeout Market while I'm usually not the biggest fan of this place bark Barbecue on the top floor is a standout exception their Dominican Texas barbecue Fusion is absolutely delicious though it's a bit on the pricey side if you're up for some American barbecue with a twist their famous brisket is worth braving the long lines bonus tip step outside onto the timeout market rooftop for one of the best free views of Manhattan after lunch I highly recommend walking the Brooklyn Bridge back to Manhattan the views are far better when you're facing the Manhattan skyline making it perfect for photos the walk takes about 30 minutes and it's absolutely worth it but if you're feeling tired or just not up for the walk no worries you can hop on the NYC fery back to Pier 11 for just $4 it's a great alternative and still offers some beautiful views along the way once you cross the East River it's just a 10minute walk to South Street Seaport a historic area filled with some of the oldest buildings in New York City it's home to the largest concentration of restored early 19th century commercial buildings making it a mustsee for history Buffs for a Hidden Gem check out Canon's walk a tucked away alley that feels like you've been transported straight back to the 19th century it's a little slice of old New York you won't want to miss if you got a little extra time head over to Pier 35 and check out the pier 35 swings it's the perfect spot to snap a photo or video with the Manhattan and Brooklyn Bridges as your backdrop trust me there's no view quite like it anywhere else in the world next hop on a Subway or grab an Uber to the Roosevelt Island tram station want to try something different check out Revel the all electric car service use this code for 70% off your first ride it's a fun eco-friendly way to get around for just the cost of a Subway swipe hop on the Roosevelt Island Tram and glide above the East River it feels like a tourist attraction but it's a regular part of City Life when you land you'll find yourself on a quiet little island that's technically part of Manhattan but feels like a world away start your exploration of Roosevelt Island with the small pox Memorial Hospital a striking example of Gothic Revival architecture that reveals a darker chapter of the Island's history you can walk the perimeter of the island or rent a city bike for a more leisurely ride other must SE stops along the way include the Franklin D Roosevelt for Freedom State Park and at the northern tip of the Island the Roosevelt Island Lighthouse if you want to spend even more time exploring the island we have a whole video dedicated to it where we spent an entire day there with a local check it out take a break and get ready for dinner if you haven't noticed by now I'm a big fan of hidden gems and sakagura is one of the best tucked away in an unassuming Midtown building and down a flight of stairs you'll discover an incredible Japanese restaurant they specialize in authentic tapa style dishes perfect for sharing and they boast an impressive selection of 260 types of sake it's a truly memorable New York experience as long as you take it easy on the sake here's an after dinner drink and entertainment option the freaky Tiki is a must for my Broadway and karaoke lovers this quirky Hawaiian themed bar not only serves up great food but also delivers a one-of-a-kind experience the host sings plays piano and takes requests plus you can hop in and sing yourself if you're in the mood for show tunes or classic oldies just slip the host a tip and you'll have the playlist at your command I want to be a big where New York in 3 days you can see a lot of NYC but if you want more amazing food options to add to your trip then click the video on the screen right now

videos.title: Why New York City is Split Into Boroughs
videos.description: Why New York City is Split Into Boroughs
videos.transcript: This is New York City. It may just be one city, but it sure doesn't feel like it. Cross a bridge or ride a few subway stops, and almost everything changes. The skyline looks different. The streets move differently. Even the cultures shift. The city is separated into burrows, and they're almost like a city within a city. People don't just say they're from New York City. They say they're from the Bronx or Queens or Brooklyn. These are neighborhoods with separate histories and in many ways separate identities. But how did this end up even happening? How did the most iconic city in the US end up with perhaps the strangest internal structure? Well, to understand that, we have to look back to a time before the five burrows were united when they were rivals and the decision to merge them was anything but inevitable. Until the very end of the 19th century, what we now call New York City didn't exist, at least not in the way we know it. Manhattan was the city of New York. The Bronx was still mostly farmland. Queens and Staten Island were made up of small towns. And Brooklyn, Brooklyn was a completely separate city. In fact, it was the third largest in America by 1860, just behind Philadelphia and the city of New York. Of course, these lines created distinct cultures. Brooklyn was seen as more suburban, more Protestant, and more middle class than the crowded, immigrant-heavy Catholic streets of Manhattan, for example. And yet, even as the region remained divided in identity and governance, some were already imagining a future where it could act as one. Since the late 1820s, there had been some discussion of a unified city. In 1857, the New York State Legislature resolved that the regions surrounding New York City should become one body to improve harbor facilities and link the systems of trade. They attempted to do so by government vote, but distrust of large government projects killed the plans before it went anywhere. Just a year later, residents of Staten Island burned down a quarantine hospital in protest of New York City's public health orders back in 1858. a sign of how resistant some burrows were to outside control. And yet, despite all that independence, the boundaries were starting to blur. The harbor connected all of them. Goods flowed through shared waterways. People were commuting across city lines every day. The industrial economy didn't stop at the rivers. By the second half of the 19th century, these cities and towns were starting to function less like neighbors and more like part of the same machine. But their systems weren't built to reflect that. Each ran its own docks, street cars, feries, and public safety departments. These separate networks made the region less efficient and more vulnerable. Business leaders and civic reformers saw it clearly. They needed a unified port, a regional government, and a single system to manage the city's future growth. Andrew Hasswell Green, known as the father of greater New York, led the effort to consolidate. It became the most ambitious municipal merger in US history. Beneath the surface, there were motives beyond just the ease of everything being unified. The state legislature in Albany was growing more powerful. But more importantly, the city of Chicago was growing rapidly on course to become the most populated city in the country. It had its railroads and Great Lakes traffic, making it the premier city of the Midwest. In 1893, it was chosen over New York as the site of the world's exhibition fair, which was a massive upset. When the fair turned out to be a massive success, Chicago became the most praised city in the country. The fear among the New York elites was that without consolidation, their city might just lose its edge or its influence. Many remembered how the power and prestige of Philadelphia, once America's financial capital, had slowly bled away once it was surpassed by New York in the late 1930s. So, the solution was quite clear. Merge the counties, centralize power, and modernize the city. But obviously not everyone actually agreed. Manhattan supported it. So did most of Queens and the Bronx. But in Brooklyn, the reaction was different. For decades, Brooklyn had prided itself on being a city in its own right. It had its own infrastructure, institutions, and a sense of identity completely separate from Manhattan. Many saw the proposed merger as a hostile takeover. In 1894, voters across the region were asked to weigh in. The results weren't even close. They were razor thin. Brooklyn voted in favor by just 277 votes, a margin of less than a quarter of a percent. In Staten Island, opposition was just as fierce. Residents feared that city governance from Manhattan would neglect their isolated burough and disrupt local control across the region. Critics warned that consolidation would dilute the homogeneous Protestant civic life, empower political machines like Tam Hall, and erase local traditions. But the promise of better water access, financial backing, and regional development eventually swayed public opinion in these two areas. Water was a big one. You see, by the 1890s, especially Brooklyn had a serious problem. The burrow had grown quickly, but its water system hadn't kept up. It relied on wells and small reservoirs in Queens, but those were starting to run dry. Residents complained about poor water pressure and dirty tap water. And to make matters worse, pumping too much ground water from underground aquifers allowed salt water to seep in from the coast, contaminating the supply. Manhattan, on the other hand, had a much better system. Its water came from the Croton Aqueduct, a 41m engineering project that carried clean water from upstate. Brooklyn didn't have anything like that. So, when the idea of consolidation came up, water was one of the biggest reasons to say yes. And in a growing industrial city, that mattered. In the end, newspapers pushed the plan. State legislators approved it and business leaders made the case that resisting the merger would mean being left behind. So on January 1st, 1898, the city of greater New York officially came into being. The term burrow was introduced to describe each of the new administrative divisions within the unified city. It had been used before in England and a few US states, but never in this way as parts of a single centralized government. It included five burs, five counties, and more than 3.4 million people, making it the second most populated city in the world at the time behind only London. In Manhattan, there was celebration. Thousands filled City Hall Park for fireworks, speeches, and the swearing in of the city's first mayor, Robert A. Van Wick. The mood was optimistic. Progress had won. But across the river, it felt more like a loss. In Brooklyn, the reaction was quiet, even bitter. Local papers described the day as ferial. A city with its own institutions, identity, and history now answered to Manhattan. Overnight, more than 40 separate municipalities were expected to fully coordinate across burrow lines. City Hall took on new authority, but burrow presidents were created to manage local interests and prevent a total centralization of power. Obviously, the infrastructure still worked like five different cities for a long time. Transportation was still a major issue. Brooklyn and Queens had grown quickly, but their links to Manhattan were limited. The subway wouldn't open until 1904, and even then, most lines only ran into Manhattan. It took decades to connect the burrows underground. Staten Island wasn't connected at all and still isn't. Though the bridges helped. Besides the Brooklyn Bridge that opened a few years earlier in 1883, the Williamsburg Bridge opened in 1903, followed by the Manhattan and Queensboro bridges in 1909. Finally, the New York City subway system unified in 1940 when the city purchased the Interbarrow Rapid Transit Company and the Brooklyn Manhattan Transit Corporation, merging them with the existing city-owned independent subway system. This brought all three major subway operating companies under city control, creating a single unified subway system. Now, the most interesting part of this unification was that of the Bronx, given it was already part of Manhattan before becoming its own burrow after consolidation. Let me explain. During the mid-9th century, the Bronx technically didn't exist in any official capacity. It was just part of the southernmost region of Westchester County, serving as a transitional zone between urban Manhattan and rural New England. Much of the land was just farmland, marshes, with a few key roads and bridges scattered about. But in 1846, the town of West Farms was carved out of it. Then came Morisania, then Kingsbridge, as the population in these areas rapidly grew. The reasons for this was simply due to its proximity to Manhattan being just a few miles north, making them ideal for commuting into the city and economic spillover. But as the city of New York kept expanding, these towns were eventually swallowed up by it in 1874. Pulled into the city's orbit as its need for land, housing, and infrastructure grew. This area became known as the annexed district, a strange in between, legally part of New York, but still geographically apart. The rest of the land east of the Bronx River followed in annexation in 1895 until the area was formally consolidated into greater New York in 1898, becoming the burrow of the Bronx. But legally, it was still part of the New York County, which included Manhattan and the Bronx together. Things started to change once the first subway line to reach the Bronx opened in 1904. Suddenly, it made living there even more cheap and easy. The population exploded, more than doubling between 1900 and 1910 as farmland quickly gave way to dense city blocks. This rapid growth created the need for better local governance. And in 1914, the Bronx finally became its own county with its own court system and administration. By 1930, it had more than 1.2 million residents. Staten Island, on the other hand, has never had any public transit connecting it to the rest of New York. There's just the Verzano narrows bridge and the fairies. It's always been a little apart though, ever since the very beginning of the US. Staten Island was one of the strongest loyalist enclaves in the colonies. So much so that British forces used it as their base of operations in 1776. George Washington even called the locals our most ineterate enemies. It became part of New York City in 1898, but the subway never came and neither did the density. For most of the 20th century, Staten Island stayed quiet, suburban, disconnected, and politically out of step with the rest of the city. The point here is that the burrows within this newly unified New York City were distinct. Technically, it was one city, but daily life told a different story. Staten Island remained semi-ural. Queens was still stitched together from old town centers. And while power was centralized in New York City Hall, the people still lived with burrow boundaries that didn't go away. Because you see, New York City may be a single municipality, but it's still five separate counties. Manhattan is New York County, Brooklyn is King's County, Queens is Queens County, the Bronx is Bronx County, and Staten Island is Richmond County. This is a legal leftover from the 1898 consolidation. The burrows were never fully absorbed into a single system. Instead, the city kept them intact politically, geographically, and culturally. Burough boundaries shaped how decisions were made. Each one developed its own political structure centered around local leaders and party organizations. There was no single machine that controlled New York City politics. After consolidation, each burrow developed its own. Tam Hall dominated Manhattan through its network of ward bosses and district leaders. Brooklyn's King's County Democrats built their power through a system of political clubs tied to neighborhoods and union halls. In Queens, the County Party grew through land deals and judicial appointments, while the Bronx was shaped by the rise of Edward J. Flynn, who became one of the most powerful Democratic figures in the country. Staten Islands organization remained smaller, but it operated within the same structure. These clubs had real control. They could decide who ran for office and who didn't. They gave out city jobs through personal connections. Loyalty mattered. If someone got a job through the club, they were expected to show up, donate, and vote the right way. The club kept track of who delivered and who didn't. That's how local leaders stayed in power. Leaders like Charles Murphy in Manhattan and John Makoule in Brooklyn set the tone for how borrow power worked inside a so-called unified city. No single figure controlled the city. Instead, power was negotiated burrow by burrow, deal by deal. If a candidate wanted to run citywide, they needed approval from every corner of the city. The machines controlled access, and the burrow lines defined the terms. That structure never really disappeared. The clubouses eventually faded, especially post 1960s, but the habits would stay in place for a long time afterwards. Bar presidents, district leaders, and county chairs still shape who runs, who wins, and what gets funded. Politics in New York has always moved through five parallel systems. It still does. That brings us to today, where each burrow still has a president, but with little governing power. It's much more symbolic these days. They don't write laws or command city agencies. The burrows are now mostly names on a map and sources of overwhelming sectarian pride for New Yorkers. The real power sits with the mayor and city council. For many residents, that structure feels distant. You vote for a burough president, but they can't fix the trains, adjust school funding, or change police priorities. But formal power isn't everything. Local resistance still matters. Sometimes more than official authority. A city-wide plan might have the mayor's backing, but if a neighborhood pushes back hard enough, it can stall or die entirely. For example, the Veritano Narrows Bridge, a critical link between Staten Island and the rest of the city, was held up for years by local opposition in Brooklyn. Bay Ridge residents fought the bridgeg's construction because it would tear through their streets, which is a fair point, and their protest delayed the project for over four decades until finally opening in 1964. Even something so clearly needed citywide was almost derailed by one burough's objections. This is how housing policy and infrastructure projects in general work in New York. The city says yes, the burrow says maybe, and the neighborhood says no. The flip side is that this is a positive, as these local resistance abilities can help curb exploitative, oftentimes politically connected development. But what this really reveals is how fractured the city still is. New York may be unified on paper, but in practice, it operates like a layered system. Burough identities remain strong. services are uneven and in many ways the original divisions still shape how the city works. Critics have long noted that city government is often exceedingly Manhattanentric with the outer burrows feeling like the poor stepchildren of city hall. That complaint that Manhattan gets the lion's share of attention and investment has echoed for over a century. Cross a bridge and things change. Your school quality, your hospital, even how quickly police show up. The city runs under one department of education. There's one NYPD and one public hospital system. But in practice, each burough feels like its own world. A 2023 report found that around 2 million New Yorkers live in what they call transit deserts, a place where reliable public transportation is far away or doesn't exist. Unsurprisingly, these deserts cluster in the outer burrows. South Brooklyn, Eastern Queens, Northern Bronx, and without fast transit, access to jobs, education, and healthcare drops, too. It's a similar narrative to what we see in states dominated by one city, to the point where over 30 Illinois counties have voted to explore seceding from the state, except the difference is that it's all about separating from just a single city. We've seen this happen a few times. Staten Island got the furthest because 65% of them actually voted to leave in 1993, but their efforts died on state constitutional grounds. Even as of recently, these talks still persist. In a way, this isn't new. The same tensions that made consolidation controversial in the 1890s, fears of losing local control, unequal representation, cultural mismatch are still here, just in different forms. The borders may have disappeared on a map, but the mindset hasn't. But this is the gift and the curse of New York City's burrows. Queens is the most linguistically diverse place on Earth. Over 160 different languages are spoken there. You can take one subway line and hear five different ones. In the Bronx, hip hop was born in a wreck room on Sedick Avenue. It shaped global culture before it was ever taken seriously by the city. Brooklyn's brownstones, bookstores, and block parties built a brand people now copy worldwide. Staten Island has the greenest space in the city with more parkland per capita than any other burrow. And Manhattan is the face of it all. The skyline, the stock exchange, Time Square, the setting of endless novels and ambitions, and of course, the concrete jungle itself. All the money, power, and iconicness a place could have. None of this came from central planning. It came from neighborhoods doing their own thing. That's the burrow system. Thank you for watching.

videos.description:
Read our full, in-depth New York City guide here: https://www.expedia.com/New-York.dx234 

First time in New York City? This is your ultimate neighborhood travel guide.
Discover the best things to do in NYC, from Midtown’s must-see landmarks to Chelsea’s creative energy, Lower Manhattan’s rich history, and Brooklyn’s scenic streets. We’ll show you the neighborhoods that capture the real spirit of New York, and why each one deserves a spot on your itinerary.


Midtown: Iconic sights, Broadway lights, and skyline views that give first-time visitors a classic New York introduction. This is where you’ll find the big hitters like Times Square, Rockefeller Center, and the Empire State Building all within easy reach.

Chelsea: The High Line, street art, and endless food finds create an easy, walkable neighborhood that lets first-timers sample New York’s creative energy. It’s perfect for soaking up galleries, markets, and elevated park views without the Midtown rush.

Lower Manhattan: Wall Street, the 9/11 Memorial, and waterfront walks offer a deep dive into the city’s history and resilience, and it’s the easiest place to catch views of the Statue of Liberty.

DUMBO: Cobblestone charm and picture-perfect skyline shots make this one of the most rewarding neighborhoods for first-timers. It has that instantly recognisable postcard view of Manhattan plus relaxed cafés and boutiques that show off Brooklyn’s vibe.

videos.transcript: Welcome to New York City. If this is your first visit, here's the perfect guide to the New York City neighborhoods that define the Big Apple. Wondering where to start, what to see, and what to do? We'll take you through the top spots every first timer needs to experience. Get ready to discover the best of New York City's neighborhoods in one video. It's your starting point for an unforgettable trip. If New York is the city of dreams, Midtown is the neighborhood where it dreams big in steel, neon, and glass. Stroll down Midtown's most iconic avenues, where skyscrapers tower over luxury storefronts and the scent of hot dogs and pretzels fills the air. It's non-stop motion New York style. Over on Park Avenue, stop by Grand Central Terminal, one of the world's most beautiful train stations. Beneath the vaulted arches, you'll feel a part of the city's constant hustle as hundreds of thousands of New Yorkers make their way through each day. Just a short walk away, Bryant Park offers the perfect contrast. In winter, it's a skating rink. In summer, it's all yoga classes, concerts, and office workers eating lunch under shady trees. With the grand facade of the New York Public Library as its backdrop, this park is the perfect stage to experience the quieter side of Midtown life. Rising 103 floors above it all is the Empire State Building. From the magnificent art deco foyer, take the elevator to the observation decks for views across the city. On a clear day, you can see up to five states. And make sure you set aside enough time to explore the exhibits on the 2nd and 80th floors. From the Empire State Building, it's a 20-minute subway ride to Central Park. There's a lot to see here, so you'll need a few hours to take in all the highlights. Spot your favorite writers and characters along the literary walk. Watch rowboats glide under Bow Bridge and soak up the music and views on Bethesda Terrace. It's only when you see the Manhattan skyline peeking over the treetops that you remember you're in one of the busiest cities in the world. Just a 15-minute walk away, you'll find the Rockefeller Center, an art deco landmark that's home to stores, restaurants, and one of New York's most photographed icons, the golden statue of Prometheus. After exploring Rockefeller Plaza, head up to the Top of the Rock Observation Deck for sweeping views of Central Park to the north and the Empire State Building and Midtown Skyline to the south. When the sun gets low, Broadway is the place to be. Grab a ticket to a show in the theater district or follow the crowds to Time Square. It's the ultimate showstopper to end your Midtown day. Now, let's head to Chelsea, a neighborhood that's equal parts creative playground and community gathering space. At Hudson Yards, check out Vessel, an eye-catching landmark where thousands of steps wind upward like a giant honeycomb. You don't even have to climb it to be impressed. From the outside, its mirrored surfaces reflect the city in amazing ways. A few blocks south, make a pit stop for lunch at Chelsea Market. This former cookie factory is now a food hall, dishing up flavors from all over the world. Between bites, browse the indie shops selling books, crafts, and vintage treasures. Once you're done, head next door to Art Tech House NYC. Here, art is transformed into an immersive experience where floors, walls, and ceilings come alive with moving light installations. Just a 3-minute walk away, you'll find the Highline, one of the city's most beloved urban reinventions. Built on an old elevated railroad, it's now a public park in the sky. A green walkway lined with community gardens, art, and amazing views. Next up, let's head to Lower Manhattan, the neighborhood where New York's story began. Stroll down Wall Street, the center of American finance and home of the New York Stock Exchange. Don't miss the Fearless Girl statue, which celebrates the power of female leadership in business. A few blocks west is the 9/11 memorial, an important place of reflection. Spend some quiet time by the twin reflecting pools, which sit in the footprints of the former World Trade Center towers. Then head underground to explore the 9/11 museum. Afterwards, stroll over to the Oculus, a shopping, art, and subway hub whose white wings symbolize peace and the city's rebirth. One of lower Manhattan's oldest and most popular neighborhoods is Little Italy. This slice of oldworld charm on Mulberry Street brims with trattorias, bakeries, and gelato shops. It's the perfect spot to linger over an espresso or a plate of fresh pasta. Or head next door to historic Chinatown, where the delicious scent of handpulled noodles and roasted duck has been filling streets since the 1850s. After lunch, head down to the East River, where the South Street Seaport blends maritime history with modern style. Watching the tall ships bobbing by Pier 16 might give you the urge to set sail yourself. Well, you're in the right neighborhood because Lower Manhattan has multiple jumping off points to Ellis Island and the Statue of Liberty. Whether you cruise by for free on the Staten Island Ferry or join a tour, saying hello to Lady Liberty is a New York City experience no one should miss. Let's cross the East River to the Brooklyn neighborhood of Dumbo. Home to two of New York's most iconic bridges. take a walk across the Brooklyn Bridge. Stretching just over a mile, this historic landmark offers sweeping views of the city in both directions. A 5-minute walk up river is the Manhattan Bridge, which first opened to traffic in 1909. Beneath this bridge is one of Dumbo's hidden gems, the Archway, a lively plaza filled with music and art installations. On weekends, it buzzes with locals browsing the Brooklyn Flea, a vintage market full of collectibles, crafts, and street food. Dumbo is filled with so many streets to explore, including one of the most photographed in the world. From the Water Street intersection, head down Washington Street, where the Manhattan Bridge is perfectly framed between the red brick warehouses. When it comes to Brooklyn's finest pizza, Dumbo delivers. Grab a slice at Grimaldi's or Juliana's Pizza, two pizzeras that have become local institutions. Or head to the food market at Empire Stores, where dozens of the city's top chefs dish up everything from burgers to bao, all with rooftop views of the Manhattan skyline. From Midtown's soaring skyscrapers to the funky streets of Dumbo, these four neighborhoods capture the best of New York for first time visitors. You'll walk through history, feast your eyes on art and architecture, and stand in spots you've seen in your favorite movies. Don't forget to share this New York guide with your travel crew, because the Big Apple is always better when it's shared.
